Summary: Cannot capture viewmarks in unsaved models
Description: If you create a new model and try to create a viewmark without saving the model, the viewmark is not saved.
Workaround: Save the model before creating viewmarks.

Summary: Figures with uitables containing strings fail to print properly to vector files such as PDF or EPS
Description: A figure with a uitable  that contains strings will not print properly to a vector graphics file such as PDF or EPS (the output is incomplete). For example, the following code creates a figure with a uitable  containing strings and saves it to a PDF file: >> fig = figure;>> t = uitable(fig,'data',{'test', 'test'; 'test5', 'test6'});>> saveas(fig,'table.pdf') The table in the output PDF file should contain the test strings, but it is empty.
Workaround: Print or export the figure using a bitmap image format, such as PNG, JPEG, or BMP.>> print -dpng table.png

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Initial value specification on Stateflow output may lead to incorrect code
Description: Initial value specification on Stateflow output may lead to incorrect code when all of these conditions are true: Stateflow output has Initial value  specification. The Stateflow output is connected to a Unit Delay block. The Initial conditions  value of the Unit Delay block is different from that of Stateflow output.?

Summary: sl_refresh_customization command does not add checks associated with custom objectives to Code Generation Advisor
Description: If you create a custom objective, add checks to this objective, and at the MATLAB command line, enter the sl_refresh_customization  command, the following bug occurs: The custom objective and associated checks are not in the Code Generation Advisor.  The Identify blocks that generate expensive fixed-point and saturation code  check is not in the Code Generation Advisor.

Summary: Using anonymous functions with blockproc in parallel mode results in increased memory usage
Description: When calling the blockproc  function, you can use anonymous function handles to specify the block function. In some cases, especially when working with large images in parallel mode, this can cause a spike in memory usage, that can freeze a system. For example, the following code might result in a large amount of memory being used (in addition to the large image) which will slow the processing significantly, when compared to the non-parallel approach. function out = tblockprocPCTfreeze  im = rand(4200000,100,2);  func = @(block_struct) sum(sum(block_struct.data,1),2);  out = blockproc(im,[73, 73],func, 'UseParallel', true);   end   ?
Workaround: To workaround this issue, use subfunctions instead of anonymous functions:function out = tblockprocPCTfreezeim = rand(4200000,100,2);func = @blockFun;out = blockproc(im,[73, 73],func, 'UseParallel', true);end?function blockData = blockFun(blockStruct)blockData = sum(sum(blockStruct.data,1),2);end??

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Unit Delay used inside enabled subsystem parent may lead to Incorrect code
Description: Incorrect code may be generated when all of the following conditions are met: 1. Unit delay block is used inside an enabled subsystem parents 2. The?enabled subsystem is configured to 'reset' for 'States when enabling' 3.? The Unit delay input is fed with a global.

Summary: Incomplete highlighting by Model Slicer
Description: Model Slicer may produce incomplete highlighting of dependencies if the model consists of any of the following:  Bus blocks Mux/Demux blocks Function-call subsystems

Summary: Model Explorer data dictionary scope node entries can be hidden when targeted for requirements navingation
Description: When a data dictionary is displayed in the Model Explorer, and there are many data entries inside one Scope node, you might not see the entry targeted by the Requirements Management Interface if it falls outside the visible range of the list view pane. Therefore, it might appear that the requirements link is not functioning. Scroll to the entry in the list to view the requirement selection.
Workaround: Install the attached patch as described in the instructions below.Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:You need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder. To save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder, open this Bug Report in a browser started in administrator mode.If you see ˇ°Error: Access is deniedˇ± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting Run as administrator.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: MATLAB crashes when calling 'close; dbquit' in debug mode.
Description: When stopped in the MATLAB debugger, executing graphics code, calling 'close; dbquit'  will cause MATLAB to crash.

Summary: MATLAB reads truncated values of int64 and uint64 CAN signals through message Signals property
Description: int64 and uint64 are not calculated to full 64-bit integer resolution when you read the Signals property of a CAN message. MATLAB reads truncated values or in some cases throws an error: Error using can.Message/get.Signals (line 474)An error occurred while getting a physical value for a signal in the message. The Vector CANdb Libraryreturned the following error code "3". This indicates "CANdbSigvalueLengthError".

Summary: Appending PostScript files is slow
Description: Starting in R2014b, appending PostScript files using the  print  function is slower than in previous releases. The time it takes to append the files increases with the size and number of files involved.
Workaround: These workarounds can improve performance:Create and append files on a local drive, instead of on a network drive.Use an external utility, such as ghostscript, to append the Postscript files, instead of using the print function with the -append option.Manually concatenate the files. You might need to include a "separator" between the files to prevent conflicts. See http://www.inkguides.com/merging-postscript-files.asp for more information.Users of  MATLAB version R2015a or R2015aSP1 can visit Bug Report ID 1349402 to download a patch that addresses this and other issues that exist in MATLAB R2015a and R2015aSP1.Users of applications compiled with MATLAB version R2015a or R2015aSP1 can visit Bug Report ID 1349473 to download a patch that addresses this and other issues in R2015a and R2015aSP1 compiled applications.

Summary: Confusing compatibility message related to variable-sized arrays
Description: If a model uses variable-sized arrays, the following error message is displayed: 'Logical indexing requires support of variable-sized arrays, which is currently disabled for code generation. Consider selecting the 'Support variable-size signals' option on the Configuration Parameters > Code Generation > Interface page.' However, selecting the recommended option does not eliminate the error. Models with variable-sized arrays are not compatible with Design Verifier.

Summary: Time Scope zooming, panning, or autoscaling in enabled or triggered subsystems show inconsistent plot
Description: If you place the Time Scope in an enabled or triggered subsystem and then disable that subsystem, the display shows blank areas. If you then zoom, pan, or autoscale the Time Scope, the blank areas are replaced with linearly interpolated lines between the enabled points.

Summary: Beeping when the Variables editor is open
Description: Error beeps occur when working with the Variables editor, or while the Variables editor is open. This happens in the following situations. A variable that is open in the Variables editor is renamed. The type of a variable that is open in the Variables editor is changed. An object that has been deleted is opened in the Variables editor.
Workaround: Close the Variables editor, and reopen it only for valid variables in the current workspace.

Summary: Display of signal hierarchy for bus can be empty?
Description: If one of the upstream blocks for a bus is a linked library block, then when you display the signal hierarchy of the bus, the display can be empty. This issue affects these three ways of viewing the signal hierarchy of a bus: Bus Selector or Bus Assignment block parameters dialog boxes Signal Hierarchy Viewer SignalHierarchy  parameter (using get_param )  ?
Workaround: Repeat the step you used to display the signal hierarchy:Bus Selector or Bus Assignment block dialog boxes -- close and reopen the dialog box.Signal Hierarchy Viewer -- deselect and reselect the signal of interest.SignalHierarchy parameter -- repeat the get_param command.?

Summary: MATLAB crashes when playing audio on NComputing desktop virtualization client
Description: MATLAB? crashes when executing the sound , audioplayer , or audiodevinfo  functions on an NComputing? remote desktop running Microsoft? Windows Server? 2008 R2. For example, this code crashes MATLAB:load handel;sound(y, Fs)

Summary: Tests that require that all elements of an input array have the same value might not generate correctly
Description: Test generation might return incorrect results when all of the following conditions are met: Your model has an input array, upon which some of the test objectives depend Those test objective(s) require that all elements of the input array have the same value The dependent test objectives are satisfied during test generation but fail to produce the expected coverage  The test generation report indicates that the objective is satisfied at a test step with all array elements having a value of dash (-), meaning that Simulink Design Verifier incorrectly determined that none of the array elements directly influence the satisfaction of test objectives

Summary: Harness model cannot be compiled after removing Signal Builder block
Description: Editing a harness model generated by Simulink Design Verifier and deleting the Signal Builder block from the harness may prevent the model from compiling. The following error is reported in the Diagnostic Viewer: Error evaluating 'InitFcn' callback of block_diagram 'MODEL'. Caused by: Operands to the || and && operators must be convertible to logical scalar values.
Workaround: Edit the Model InitFcn Callback. From the Callback tab of File -> Model Properties -> Model Properties, delete the line: sldvshareprivate('parameters','init',[],0);from the callback.

Summary: For large coordinate values of junctions '.Position.Center' API returns invalid answer.
Description: The '.Position.Center' property of a junction can return an invalid result due to interger overflow. It is possible for the coordinates of a junction in the Stateflow diagram to exceed the maximum value for a 16 bit integer.
Workaround: Download the attachment and extract the contents to somewhere on your MATLAB path. The included function, getJunctionPosition, can be used in place of querying the .Position.Center property.>> [x,y] = getJunctionPosition(h);where h is the handle to the Stateflow Junction.

Summary: When using every temporal logic, Stateflow is unable to build a standalone executable for simulation in Rapid Accelerator mode
Description: If you use the every  temporal logic operator on a transition in a Stateflow chart and try to simulate the model in Rapid Accelerator mode, MATLAB incorrectly displays the following error message: Code generation and simulation targets registered different sets of persistent variables for the block. External or Rapid Accelerator mode simulation requires code generation and simulation targets to register the same set of persistent variables for this block. This discrepancy is typically caused by MATLAB functions that have different code paths for simulation and code generation targets where these code paths define different sets of persistent variables. Please identify these code paths in the offending block and rewrite the MATLAB code so that the set of persistent variables is the same between simulation and code generation.
Workaround: In R2015a, you can work around this issue by turning off Stateflow debugging.>> sfc coder_options forceDebugOff 1Note that this turns off animation and debugging for all Stateflow charts in the current MATLAB session.This will also prevent you from setting breakpoints, which could complicate debugging.There are no known workarounds for prior releases.

Summary: MATLAB might become unresponsive when using an NI-VISA connection
Description: When you connect to your instrument using a National Instruments VISA interface (NI-VISA) connection, calling the visa  interface fclose  function can cause MATLAB to become unresponsive under the following conditions: You have multiple vendor VISA installations (such as NI-VISA,  Keysight/Agilent IO Libraries, TekVISA, or R&S VISA) NI-VISA is the primary vendor
Workaround: Uninstall all other vendor VISA libraries except NI-VISA, orUse a different vendor VISA library

Summary: MATLAB might crash resulting in a log mentioning inPathNotification::function_delete_notification
Description: MATLAB might crash resulting in a crash log that contains the following information: Stack Trace (from fault):[  0] 0x0321cbd3 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00052179 inPathNotification::function_delete_notification+00009411[  1] 0x032798d1 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00432337 inGetSeqVarPtrsFromCurrentWS+00083585[  2] 0x0327b431 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00439345 inGetSeqVarPtrsFromCurrentWS+00090593[  3] 0x032230b8 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00078008 inPathNotification::function_delete_notification+00035240[  4] 0x0324bed5 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00245461 inPathNotification::function_delete_notification+00202693[  5] 0x032430eb C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00209131 inPathNotification::function_delete_notification+00166363[  6] 0x03242144 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00205124 inPathNotification::function_delete_notification+00162356[  7] 0x032e5dea C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00876010 inIs_ws_variable+00001306[  8] 0x0316e508 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_dispatcher.dll+00058632 Mfh_file::dispatch_fh_impl+00000600[  9] 0x0316e2ab C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_dispatcher.dll+00058027 Mfh_file::dispatch_fh+00000027[ 10] 0x0316e9b4 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_dispatcher.dll+00059828 Mfunction_handle::dispatch+00000692 The exact steps needed to reproduce this crash are not yet known.

Summary: Plotting noisy data can result in a jagged line with unexpected noise
Description: When plotting noisy data, the resulting line appears jagged. Also, there appears to be more noise in the line than expected from the data. The unexpected noise occurs because the line object is created using multiple smaller line segments joined together using a miter join.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect numerical result from generated code for model step function output that is updated in conditional execution context
Description: When building a model for which the model step function has been customized using C function prototype control or C++ class interface control, incorrect code might be generated if both of the following conditions are true: A scalar output of the model step function is configured to be passed by value. (In the model function or C++ class interface configuration dialog box, Category  is set to Value .) During execution, the output is updated in a conditionally executed context. Examples of a conditionally executed context include the following: The output has a slower sample time than the fundamental rate of the model when the model is used as a referenced model. The output is written to in a conditionally executed subsystem. The output is written to by an S-function that might conditionally update the output. The output is written to by a Hit Crossing block. The output is written to by a Rate Transition block.    Under these conditions, executing the generated C or C++ code might produce numerical results that do not match the model simulation results. In release R2011a or later of MATLAB, you can configure a diagnostic that might trigger a warning or error message at code generation time for some of the conditions. In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, set the diagnostic Diagnostics > Model Referencing > Invalid root Inport/Outport block connection  to warning  or error . However, the diagnostic does not apply to top models, and for referenced models, it might not detect all possible scenarios that produce incorrect numerical results.
Workaround: To work around this bug: If your model has a custom model step function, for which a scalar output is passed by value and is updated in a conditionally executed context, configure the output to be passed by pointer. In the model function or C++ class interface configuration dialog box, set Category to Pointer.

Summary: NullPointerException in Linux terminal window after exiting MATLAB
Description: On some Linux distributions, a NullPointerException may appear in the Linux terminal window when exiting MATLAB: Exception in thread "Thread-346" java.lang.NullPointerException  at com.teamdev.jxbrowser.chromium.internal.ipc.IPC.b(Unknown Source)  at com.teamdev.jxbrowser.chromium.internal.ipc.IPC.shutdown(Unknown Source)  at com.teamdev.jxbrowser.chromium.internal.aq.run(Unknown Source)  at java.lang.Thread.run(Unknown Source)  This exception is harmless, and is the result of a failure to create a Chromium-based browser. When this happens, MATLAB automatically falls back to a Mozilla-based browser and continues functioning.
Workaround: To avoid seeing this exception, set your default HTML renderer to use Mozilla rather than Chromium by running this command in a Linux terminal window:>> com.mathworks.mlwidgets.html.HtmlComponentFactory.setDefaultType('HTMLPANEL');

Summary: Incorrect read data from generated IP core AXI4 interface
Description: A read access to the AXI4 slave interface of a generated IP core can return incorrect data if the AXI4 master interface deasserts the RREADY signal during the read access. For example, the AXI4 master interface may deassert the RREADY signal due to backpressure from external memory, or when the AXI4 master interface and AXI4 slave interface are running on different clock domains. In this situation, the read access returns data from the previously specified address.
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.For each file that appears in the .zip file, find the corresponding file in your existing <MATLAB> folder and make a backup of it. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:You need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder. To save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder, open this Bug Report in a browser started in administrator mode.If you see ˇ°Error: Access is deniedˇ± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting Run as administrator.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: MATLAB might crash resulting in a log mentioning mxArray_tag::get_kernel_bits
Description: MATLAB might crash resulting in a crash log that contains the following information: Stack Trace (from fault):[  0] 0x00000000fe10f0c0 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\libmx.dll+00061632 mxArray_tag::get_kernel_bits+00000000[  1] 0x0000000010bd8418 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00099352 in_is_matlab_function+00043976[  2] 0x0000000010bda321 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00107297 in_is_matlab_function+00051921[  3] 0x0000000010ca684f C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00944207 MathWorks::MException::MExceptionEnvelope::MCOSClassID+00000991[  4] 0x0000000010ca67b1 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00944049 MathWorks::MException::MExceptionEnvelope::MCOSClassID+00000833[  5] 0x0000000010c1da75 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00383605 inGetCurrentWS+00155269[  6] 0x0000000010c1ce8c C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2015a\bin\win64\m_interpreter.dll+00380556 inGetCurrentWS+00152220 The exact steps needed to reproduce this crash are not yet known.

Summary: Unable to control text properties for contour labels when using the clabel function
Description: You can no longer set text properties if you pass the contour object handle h  as an input argument to the function. For example, this code does not set the font size: clabel(C,h,'Fontsize',14)  Additionally, if you pass the contour object handle h  as an input argument you cannot return text object handles in order to adjust text properties after calling clabel . For example, this code warns and returns an empty array:tl = clabel(C,h)  These behaviors are described in the reference page for the clabel  function in Releases R2014b and R2015a.
Workaround: To work around these limitations:Log into your computer using an account with Administrator privileges.If you are using R2014b, download the attached file,workaround_1114747-14b_2015-06-03.zipOr, if you are using R2015a, download the attached file, workaround_1114747-15a_2015-06-03.zipOn Windows, double-click the file to view its contents, then click the Extract button. On Macintosh, double-click the file to extract its contents. On Linux, use the unzip command.Complete the instructions in the enclosed text file, README.txtThe workaround will allow you to control any of the following text properties via name-value inputs to the clabel function even when the contour handle is included as an input:BackgroundColorColorEdgeColorFontAngleFontNameFontSize (specified in points)FontSmoothingFontWeightInterpreterLineStyleLineWidthMarginOther text properties, such as Children, will result in a warning at the time clabel is called, or an error if they are read-only (like Type, for example). For further details, type "help clabel" at the command prompt after installing the workaround.If you wish, you can add and modify text property settings via multiple calls to clabel, as in this example:   [x,y,z] = peaks;   [C,h] = contour(x,y,z);   clabel(C,h,'FontSize',15,'Color','red')   clabel(C,h,'FontWeight','bold','Color','blue')   clabel(C,h,'FontSize','default')Using multiple calls to the clabel function will allow you to change text properties to customize your contour plot without needing text object handles.

Summary: Small line widths (less than 1 point) do not print correctly when using OpenGL
Description: Small line widths (less than 1 point) do not print or save correctly when printing or saving with the OpenGL renderer. For example, this code plots three lines with different line widths and saves the figure to foo.png. Open foo.png to view the saved line widths. plot([1:4],ones(4,1),'r', 'LineWidth', .9);hold on;plot([1:4]+3,ones(4,1),'g','LineWidth',1.8);plot([1:4]+6,ones(4,1),'b','LineWidth',3.6);legend('Width 0.9','Width 1.8','Width 3.6');print -dpng foo.png
Workaround: You can visit Bug Report ID 1349402 to download a patch that addresses this and other issues that exist in MATLAB R2015a and R2015aSP1.You can visit Bug Report ID 1349473 to download a patch that addresses this and other issues in R2015a and R2015aSP1 compiled applications.

Summary: Job status not synchronized between Polyspace UI and Polyspace Server
Description: When launching an analysis in batch mode, the following error message can appear when the analysis is sent to the server. Error: remote verification with ID null is not accessible on server "server name"  In many cases, this message is incorrect and the analysis was sent successfully to the server. You can use the Polyspace Job Monitor to check the real status of analysis on the server. If the analysis is running successfully on the server, this message is only a synchronization issue between the Polyspace UI and the scheduler on the server.
Workaround: Installation instructions (R2014a, R2014b or R2015a)Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your Polyspace installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your Polyspace installation. By default this is in c:\Program Files\MATLAB or in /usr/local/MATLAB on Linux systems. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps. polyspace is one of the principal sub-folders of <MATLAB>.Close the Polyspace User Interface (UI).Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux Systems: Type the following in a shell:unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux Systems: Type the following in a shell: cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Check that binaries have appropriate ˇ®read & executeˇŻ permissions.Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.Reopen the Polyspace UI.

Summary: MATLAB may crash when resizing Simulink or MuPad windows using the Windows Snap feature
Description: Resizing Simulink or MuPad windows using the Windows Snap feature may cause MATLAB to crash in high-resolution screens.  The resulting crash stack trace will have the following component: QLineF::operator==  The Snap feature enables resizing windows via dragging them to the edges of the screen (i.e. drag to the top to maximize the window). This problem has been reported by a small number of customers. MathWorks has not been able to reproduce the problem. We suspect that the problem involves a third-party installed application interfering with one of our core libraries.
Workaround: A possible way to work around this behavior is to use keyboard shortcuts instead of the Windows Snap feature.  For example, to maximize a window you can press on the Start and Up Arrow keys while the window is on focus.

Summary: Simulink can crash when interacting with the Explorer bar while looking under a mask
Description: If you click inside the Simulink Editor Explorer bar while looking under the mask of a masked subsystem, and then press the ESC key, Simulink can crash. The stack trace generated will contain references to GLEE::QtViewModel2::rowFromUID , for example: [  0] 0x00000000e01148bb C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2014b\bin\win64\glee2_qt.dll+01132731 GLEE::QtViewModel2::rowFromUID+00000155[  1] 0x00000000e0112cdf C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2014b\bin\win64\glee2_qt.dll+01125599 GLEE::QtViewModel2::indexFromUIDs+00000095[  2] 0x00000000e00d8ec4 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2014b\bin\win64\glee2_qt.dll+00888516 GLEE::QtAbstractView2::doScrollTo+00000116...

Summary: Calling coder.updateBuildInfo in a System Object with boolean parameter values may cause an error
Description: Calling coder.updateBuildInfo  in a System Object with boolean parameter values may cause the following error:  Incorrect arguments have been passed to: RTW.BuildInfo.addLinkObjects Please refer to the help on this method for more information. An example of a call that error out is: coder.updateBuildInfo('addLinkObjects',libName, libPath, '', true, false);

Summary: MATLAB can crash at startup when using Ctrl+C
Description: MATLAB can crash during startup when trying to respond to a Ctrl+C  event. The resulting crash log contains the following ctrlCProcessed  function at or near the top of the stack trace
Workaround: Wait until the command window prompt has appeared before using Ctrl+CIf you have encountered this issue because MATLAB is taking too long to startup, please contact technical support for additional assistance.

Summary: Size of MATLAB Desktop decreases after opening a Simulink model or using some MATLAB toolboxes
Description: On Windows? computers with a high definition display (high DPI mode), the MATLAB? Desktop decreases in size when you launch Simulink? or MuPAD?, or use capabilities of some toolboxes such as the MATLAB Compiler. Subsequently, some GUIs might also open with a distorted layout, small icons, and fonts. Some user actions that rely on Simulink functionality or the creation of System Objects? also cause the size of the MATLAB Desktop to decrease.
Workaround: Installation instructionsFor R2015a (Windows ONLY):  Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report.  Extract the files from the .zip file.  Launch MATLAB and navigate to extracted files.  Run toggleHighDPISupport (the .p file).  Restart MATLAB.  Repeat the previous two steps to toggle high DPI support on and off.The workaround fixes the automatic resizing of the MATLAB Desktop on high DPI displays as explained in the Description section. However other standalone MATLAB windows (e.g., the Help Browser) may still contain small icons and text.  To enlarge the fonts in the Help Browser, use the Ctrl+"+" keys. You can also open the context menu by right-clicking and select the Zoom In menu item.

Summary: Variables editor throws java.lang.NullPointerException if structure has uninitialized field
Description: The Variables editor throws a java.lang.NullPointerException when it tries to open a structure with an uninitialized field. A field is uninitialized when its value is assigned by an expression that does not return a value. For example: clear variables  c = {1};  s(1).f = c{false};  The structure s  has a field f  with an uninitialized value. If you open s  in the Variables editor, MATLAB throws an exception.
Workaround: View or edit structures in the Command Window instead of the Variables editor.

Summary: Help browser is unable to open on some Linux? machines
Description: When attempting to open the MATLAB? Help browser on some Linux machines, the browser fails to open, and this message appears in the Command Window:Exception in thread "XPCOMMessageLoop" java.lang.UnsatisfiedLinkError:/usr/local/MATLAB/R2013b/sys/jxbrowser/glnxa64/xulrunner/xulrunner-linux-64/libxul.so: dlopen: cannot load any more object with static TLS
Workaround: At the MATLAB command prompt, type the following, and then restart MATLAB. (Be aware that issuing this command results in a degraded appearance of documentation pages and loss of some functionality.)webutils.htmlrenderer('basic');MATLAB remembers this setting across sessions.To reset the browser to its default setting, type this command at the MATLAB command prompt:webutils.htmlrenderer('default');

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: Simulation behavior and generated code behavior might differ when Variant Subsystems are configured to generate preprocessor conditionals and model sample times are under specified
Description: The results you see in generated code might not match the results observed during simulation, if both these conditions are satisfied. Your model contains a Variant Subsystem block that is configured to generate preprocessor conditionals. Your model contains underspecified sample times.
Workaround: Ensure that the sample times of your model are not underspecified. To avoid sample time mismatch between simulation and code generation, specify the sample time on the Inport and Outport blocks of the variant subsystem.

Summary: Incorrect drawing order in 3D rendering with Painters renderer
Description: Rendering 3D scenes of intersecting graphics objects can cause MATLAB to draw objects in the incorrect order when using the Painters renderer. The incorrect order can occur under the following scenarios: Two graphics objects intersect within an axes A graphics object intersects the axes boundary  The incorrect order can occur in figures with a Renderer  property set to 'painters' . It can also occur in a file printed using a vector graphic file output such as .EPS or .PDF. The following are examples of this behavior: 1. Line segment and polygon intersection p = patch([0 1 1 0],[0 0 1 1],'r');patch([0 1 1 0],[0 0 1 1],'r');line([0.4 0.6],[0.5 0.5],[-1 1]);view(3);set(gcf,'Renderer','painters');  Notice that the part of the line segment that is occluded by the patch is still visible. Change the figure's renderer to opengl to see the correct result. 2. Graphics object intersecting with axes box a = [0 0]; b = [0 1]; c = [1 1];s = [NaN NaN];x = [a; c; s; b; b; s; a; a; s; a; c; s; b; b; s; c; c];y = [b; b; s; a; a; s; a; c; s; b; b; s; c; c; s; a; c];z = [a; a; s; a; c; s; b; b; s; c; c; s; a; c; s; b; b];surface(x,y,z,'CData',rand(17,2));axis tight;view(3);set(gcf,'Renderer','painters'); Notice that the back face of the box can be seen through the side of the box.
Workaround: For rendering-only, set the figure renderer to OpenGL by executing the following command:set(gcf,'Renderer','OpenGL');For printing to vector graphics file outputs consider the following:Split objects that intersect within an axesSet axes limits to avoid intersection of objects with the axes box

Summary: Persistent variables can get wrong values in Interpreted MATLAB Function block
Description: When a MATLAB function using persistent variables is executed by an Interpreted MATLAB Function block, it can produce wrong numerical results.This happens when the value of the Output dimensions  parameter is -1 . For example, if a persistent variable is designed to count how many times the function is called during a simulation, the end result will be one more than the expected answer.
Workaround: To work around this problem, specify a value other than -1 for the Output dimensions parameter to prevent the Interpreted MATLAB Function block from calling your function before simulation.In general, the Interpreted MATLAB Function block is designed for simple MATLAB expressions or functions. For complex cases, use the MATLAB Function block instead.

Summary: Nonlinear solvers do not accept x0 as a 3-or-higher-dimensional array.
Description: In R2012b and later, nonlinear solvers do not accept objective and nonlinear constraint functions having input variable, x , of dimension higher than two. The dimension of the initial point x0  gives the dimension of x .
Workaround: Define x0 as a column vector x0 = x0(:). Within the objective and nonlinear constraint functions, reshape x to the desired size. For example,x = reshape(x,dimension1,dimension2,dimension3,dimension4); At the end of the functions, reshape x back to a column vector via x = x(:);

Summary: Assignment to an observable property through nested cell content indexing can cause memory corruption.
Description: When assigning to an observable property through nested cell content indexing, the bug can cause a value in a cell array to be overwritten with an empty double array. For example, if:var.cellarray1{1}.cellarray2{1}  is a handle object and var.cellarray1{1}.cellarray2{1}.prop  is an observable property of the handle object, the following assignment statement,var.cellarray1{1}.cellarray2{1}.prop = 1; can cause the cell arrayvar.cellarray1{1}.cellarray2{1}  to be overwritten with an empty double array [].
Workaround: To work around this issue, avoid using cell content indexing on the left side of the assignment. Instead, break the assignment into two or more separate statements. For example, break the previous indexing assignment apart like this:h = var.cellarray1{1}.cellarray2{1};h.prop = 1;

Summary: Multiple level of property references involving constant properties or methods may produce wrong result.
Description: Multiple dot references involving constant properties or methods, such as a.b.c.d  where either b , c , or d  is a constant property, may produce wrong result.
Workaround: Disable the JIT for the function that produces wrong result using   feature scopedaccelenablement off For example, to disable the JIT for the function bad_fun, use   function r = bad_fun(a)     feature scopedaccelenablement off;     r = a.b.c.d;   end

Summary: Constant properties that contain structs and cells do not expand as comma-separated lists in dot and curly brace indexing expressions
Description: If you have a class with a constant property whose value is a struct or cell, and attempt to generate a comma-separated list using that property in a dot or curly brace indexing expression, the expression returns only the first element of the struct or cell.For example, consider this class definition: classdef SampleClass    properties ( Constant )        StructProp = struct('Field', { 'F1', 'F2', 'F3' });        CellProp = {'C1', 'C2', 'C3'};    endend The expression[SampleClass.StructProp.Field]  should concatenate all the fields of StructProp and yield the string 'F1F2F3' . However, it yields 'F1' . Further,[SampleClass.CellProp{1:3}] should yield the string 'C1C2C3' , but instead yields 'C1' .
Workaround: Copy the constant property to a local variable, and then apply the dot or curly brace indexing operation to the variable. For example,f = SampleClass.StructProp;[f.Field]orc = SampleClass.CellProp;[c{1:3}]

Summary: Graphics in a figure appear blurry on Mac systems with Retina display?
Description: When viewing graphics in MATLAB on a Mac computer with a Retina display, the graphics appear blurry. On a Retina display, the width and height of a figure contains twice as many pixels as the same size figure on a non-Retina display.  Graphics on the Retina display are stretched across the extra pixels, causing a blurry appearance. ?

Summary: Break points are not restored from a struct under special conditions.
Description: Under certain conditions, not all breakpoints are restored from a struct. This happens when you set breakpoints in a function call hierarchy across multiple files. This problem occurs when you collect breakpoints in a struct, clear all the breakpoints, and then attempt to restore breakpoints from the struct. Not all breakpoints are restored. The following example illustrates the problem. Consider functions myfun1 , myfun2 , myfun3 , and myclearfun : function myfun1disp myfun1-a myfun2 myclearfun disp myfun1-b function myfun2 disp myfun2-a disp myfun2-b myfun3;function myfun3disp myfun3-adisp myfun3-b function myclearfun disp myclearfun-a s = dbstatus('-completenames'); br save s.mat s; br dbclear all; clear classes; pause(0.01); load s.mat; dbstop(s); disp myclearfun-b  Now consider the script file: dbstop in myfun1 at 3 dbstop in myfun1 at 5 dbstop in myfun2 at 3 dbstop in myfun2 at 5 dbstop in myfun3 at 3 dbstop in myfun3 at 5dbstop in myclearfun at 3 dbstop in myclearfun at 5 dbstop in myclearfun at 10 myfun1  Running the above script and inspecting the files in the MATLAB editor shows that only breakpoints in myfun2  and myfun3  are restored.
Workaround: Avoid this situation by saving the breakpoints before running the code. Restore the breakpoints as a separate step by using the saved breakpoints as a script.

Summary: The DEAL function produces an error if an expression's left-hand side has multiple variables with colon indexing
Description: DEAL cannot properly assign values if the output contains multiple variables with colon indexing. The following code illustrates the problem.    a(1).x1 = 1;    a(1).x2 = 2;    a(2).x1 = 33;    a(2).x2 = 44;    b(1).x1 = [];   b(1).x2 = [];   b(2).x1 = [];   b(2).x2 = [];   [b(:).x1,b(:).x2] = deal(a(:).x1, a(:).x2);??? Insufficient outputs from right hand side to satisfy comma separated list expansion on left hand side.  Missing [] are the most likely cause.
Workaround: Construct a syntax that does not use colon indexing on the left-hand side. Using the same definitions of a and b from the description, the following command should not throw an error:   [b.x1,b.x2] = deal(a(:).x1,a(:).x2);

Summary: MATLAB can crash when calling a nested function from an anonymous function handle
Description: MATLAB can crash when calling a nested function from an anonymous function handle, as shown in the following code: function x = nf(y) function a = nested(b)  a = b * 2; end fh = @(x)nested(x); for i=1:10   [fh,x] = bar(fh, y); endendfunction [fh,x] = bar(fh, y) for i=1:10   [fh,x] = baz(fh, y); endendfunction [fh,x] = baz(fh, y) function a = nested(b)  a = b + z; end z = [1 2]; x = fh(y); fh = @(x)nested(x);end
Workaround: Make the function unnested or use feature('scopedAccelEnablement','off') in the function that creates the anonymous function handle. function x = nf(y)  function a = nested(b)   a = b * 2;  end feature('scopedAccelEnablement','off');  fh = @(x)nested(x);  for i=1:10    [fh,x] = bar(fh, y);  end end function [fh,x] = bar(fh, y)  for i=1:10    [fh,x] = baz(fh, y);  end end function [fh,x] = baz(fh, y)  function a = nested(b)   a = b + z;  end  z = [1 2];  x = fh(y);  fh = @(x)nested(x); end

Summary: Crash when indexing with end into a temporary result in an expression that uses a built-in name as a variable
Description: If you define a variable that uses a built-in name, and use that in an expression that indexes into a temporary result at the command line using the end  keyword, MATLAB can crash. For example:x = {1};set = [1 2 3];       % set is the name of a built-inset > x{1,1}(end)
Workaround: Avoid using the names of built-ins for variables (such as set). For example:s = [1 2 3];s > x{1,1}(end)Alternatively, do not index into temporary results with end (such as x{1,1}(end)). For example:tmp = x{1,1};set > tmp(end)Note: To determine whether a name is built-in, use the which function. For example,which setreturnsbuilt-in (matlabroot/toolbox/matlab/graphics/set)

Summary: A combination of parfor, anonymous functions, nested functions, and MCOS can lead to reading of the wrong variable
Description: In rare cases, the combined use of parfor, anonymous functions, nested functions, and MCOS can cause a variable reference to evaluate to a value held by a different variable.
Workaround: You can avoid this error by removing the use of one of these language features. The easiest is to change an anonymous function into a local or nested function.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: Simulation behavior and generated code behavior might differ when subsystem variants use preprocessor conditions
Description: Simulation behavior and generated code behavior for a model might differ when the model contains variant subsystems that use preprocessor conditions. This can occur even when the same variants are active in both simulation and code execution. The behavior differences result from differences in the propagation of derived attributes, such as sample times or data types.
Workaround: To work around this bug, perform the following steps:To make the propagation of derived attributes consistent between simulation and code execution, place Signal Specification blocks before and after the inports and outports of variant subsystems, and use the blocks to lock down the following attributes:Data typeDimensionSample timeSignal typeSampling modeTo enforce the sample time specifications, go to the Diagnostics > Sample Time pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box and set the parameter Enforce sample times specified by Signal Specification blocks to error.

Summary: Using global variables in expressions that call functions that modify that global variable may cause unexpected results.
Description: Using global variables in expressions that call functions that modify that global variable may produce unexpected results. The following example function incx  increments global variable x  and returns its new value:     function r = incx    global x;    x = x + 1;    r = x; The third line of the code shown below calls this function. However, this same statement x + incx , also uses the value of x  in computing the answer. The program returns the incorrect value of 4 instead of the expected value, 3:     global x;    x = 1;    x + incx    ans =          4             % The answer should be 3
Workaround: To work around this problem, you can store global variables in temporary variables before using them in this manner:    global x;    x = 1;    % Copy x into a non-global variable, and then call incx.    y = x;    y + incx    ans =          3

Summary: MATLAB desktop appears fuzzy and the command get(0,screensize) doesn't return the correct screen size.
Description: On Vista and Windows 7, this'll happen when the DPI (dots per inch) setting is greater than 100% and the option "Use Windows XP style DPI scaling" is not set. For some themes such as "Windows Classic" this option is set automatically, and thus preventing this problem.  MATLAB is not yet what is called "High DPI Aware". This means it does not honor DPI settings. By default, Windows itself will scale up the MATLAB desktop as best it can. The way in which the OS does this results in this problem. When the "Use Windows XP style DPI scaling" option is set, the OS stops this behavior. All application are then expected to properly honor the DPI setting. However, since MATLAB is not High DPI Aware, MATLAB windows may not scale properly when this option is set. So the user is trading one problem for another.
Workaround: Set the "Use Windows XP style DPI scaling" option. On Windows 7 do:Click the Windows  "Start" button in the lower left corner of the screen. At the bottom of the pop-up menu that appears, enter "display"  in the "Search progams and files" field and then hit "Enter". Select "Display" from the "Control Panel" results at appear within the menu.Select "Set custom text size (DPI)" in the panel to the left.Check the "Use Windows XP style DPI scaling" box at the bottom of the "Custom DPI Setting" pop-up.Select OK.Select "Apply".Select "Log off now".Log back on.

Summary: MATLAB crashes during debugging
Description: In rare instances, MATLAB may crash while debugging programs involving structs or objects. If MATLAB crashes while debugging such a program and leaves a stack trace including a function called inFlowAnalyzeHotSegment, it may be an occurrence of this particular bug.
Workaround: There are three possible workarounds (ordered in terms of possible loss of performance vs. likelihood of resolving the issue):Disable optimization in the offending program file using the command "feature scopedAccelEnablement off".Disable the optimization of structures using the command "feature jitallow structs off".Disable optimization completely using the command "feature accel off".If none of these workarounds solve the problem, then there is a different bug causing the problem.?

Summary: Creating many variables using functions like EVAL, ASSIGNIN, EVALIN, or LOAD causes an error
Description: When I try to create a large number of variables in the workspace using EVAL, as per the following code: for k = 1:100000    eval(['x' num2str(k) '= 1;']);end I receive an error message similar to: ??? Error using ==> evalThe current workspace already has too many variables; there is no room for "x65526". I receive a similar error when I try to create variables using EVALIN or ASSIGNIN instead of EVAL, or when I try to LOAD a MAT-file containing a large number of variables.

Summary: MATLAB calls overloaded subsref methods with incorrect nargout when evaluating indexing expressions that are not assigned to a variable
Description: MATLAB calls overloaded subsref  methods with a nargout  equal to 0 when evaluating any indexing expression that does not solely contain dot-indexing operations, and that is not explicitly assigned to a variable. For example:     a(1).b.Field   a.b(:).Field   c{1}.a.b.Field It does not matter if the object is directly indexed via parentheses/braces or not. For example, in the first case above, "a " could be a structure and a(1).b  could contain an object of a class with an overloaded subsref . In that case, the object itself would only be dot-indexed, but nargout  will nevertheless be incorrect.

Summary: Indexing into result of a class's static method call fails to error in some situations.
Description: Indexing into a static method call errors (correctly) in a script MATLAB file, but not in a function MATLAB file or at the command prompt. For example, consider the file, methodindex.m, composed of the following MCOS class: classdef methodindex     properties         x = 0.0;         y = 0.0;     end          methods (Static)         function result = getInstance(x1)             result = methodindex();             result.x = x1;         end     end end  Consider a script MATLAB file, ascript.m, composed of just the expression: methodindex.getInstance(2).x At the MATLAB prompt, compare the results for:    >> methodindex.getInstance(2).x   ans =        2 and    >> ascript   ??? Input argument "x1" is undefined.   Error in ==> methodindex>methodindex.getInstance at 11        result.x = x1;   Error in ==> ascript at 1   methodindex.getInstance(2).x The command line case should produce an error like the script MATLAB file case. MATLAB does not support direct follow on indexing into the results of any function call.
Workaround: Use a temporary. For the example above, replace   methodindex.getInstance(2).xwith   tmp = methodindex.getInstance(2);   tmp.x

Summary: The -delegate flag to mpiexec does not work correctly on machines running 64-bit Windows operating systems
Description: When using LSF or PBS Pro schedulers on cluster machines running 64-bit Microsoft Windows operating systems, the parallel job launch mechanism using delegation does not work correctly. The job might appear to hang or to fail. This is caused by a bug in the MPICH2 implementation.
Workaround: The simplest workaround is to use the "nodelegate" configuration. However, this relies on the user credentials being available on each cluster node. You can add the user credentials to a node by executing mpiexec -register on that node, and following the prompts.

Summary: Comma-separated assignment to a structure does not work.
Description: Comma-separated assignment to multiple structure arrays, each of which represents multiple values, does not work. The following example demonstrates the problem:    S = struct('married', {true false false true});   C = {'joe', 'mary', 'jane', 'pete';   ...         36,    24,     32,     54}';   [S(1:4).name, S(1:4).age] = C{1:4,1:2}
Workaround: There are two possible work-arounds. One is to assign one structure at a time:   [S(1:4).name] = C{1:4,1};   [S(1:4).age] = C{1:4,2};The other possibility is to assign multiple structures, but one element at a time. The following statement assigns to S(3):   [S(3).name, S(3).age] = C{3,1:2};

Summary: Running out of memory can cause MATLAB to exit under certain circumstances.
Description: MATLAB requires that a certain amount of memory be available in order to throw an exception using the MException class. If that memory is not available, the allocation of the MException may fail, causing MATLAB to exit. This is most likely to occur if your programs incrementally allocate small amounts of memory.
Workaround: There are currently no known work-arounds.

Summary: Functions may suffer a performance hit if they use variables defined in a script.
Description: Given the following three files, foo.m runs much faster than script.m because foo's variables are defined in the same function: % foo.m    function foo    a=1; b=2; c=3; d=4;    for k = 1:1e6        a = b*c*sqrt(d);    end  % script.m    setup;    for k = 1:1e6        a = b*c*sqrt(d);    end  % setup.m    a=1; b=2; c=3; d=4;
Workaround: If possible, don't use scripts to initialize local variables used in functions.

Summary: MATLAB? Profiler appears to count idle time when called from the command line using the CPU timer
Description: When you use the CPU timer (that is, the -timer cpu  option), the MATLAB? Profiler scales charged CPU time to the total time during which the Profiler was active for the purpose of converting CPU clock ticks to seconds. For example, suppose you turn the Profiler on, wait 8 seconds, run 2 seconds worth of code, and then turn the Profiler off - 10 seconds have passed. If this equates to 1000 clock ticks (100 ticks/second, a ratio which varies from system to system), then roughly 200 clock ticks of CPU time are charged to MATLAB. The Profiler scales charged ticks by a ratio of 200 clock ticks to 10 seconds, thus using a 20 ticks/second scale, which is incorrect, and therefore a bug. The times reported by the Profiler are self-consistent, relative to other times reported in the same run, but the absolute values are incorrect. Note that, despite appearances, the Profiler is not including the time that elapses when the CPU is idle, because when the CPU is idle, no CPU cycles are charged to the running process.
Workaround: To work around the bug, do either one of the following:Enable and disable the profiler immediately before and after the call to the profile function, as shown in the following example:profile on -timer cpu; runCodeToProfile; profile viewerUse the real option to -timer:profile on -timer real;

Summary: Indexing into an array with the colon operator and subscripts of type CHAR does not work
Description: If you index into an array using the colon operator with character (char) operands, the indexing operation either returns an empty result (for indexed references), or assigns no values (for indexed assignment). For example, the following should set the 97th, 98th, and 99th elements of A to 1, because those are the ASCII values for 'a', 'b', and 'c'. Instead, no change is made to A. % Indexed Assignment A('a':'c') = 1;   The following should store the 97th, 98th, and 99th elements of A in B. Instead, B is assigned an empty (1-by-0) array: % Indexed ReferenceB = A('a':'c'); ?
Workaround: The simplest workaround is to enclose the index in square brackets, like this:A(['a':'c']) = 1;andB = A(['a':'c']);?
